target.title: fix(gateway): honor signed node ids
target.body:
## Linked context

Closes #61569.

## Summary

Fixes #61569.

- Honors explicit `openclaw node run --node-id ...` values across node pairing, registry, catalog/listing, pending ack/pull, node events, and invoke-result identity checks.
- Treats the CLI node id as trusted only when it is covered by the device-auth signature.
- Binds paired custom node ids to the authenticated node device with `ownerDeviceId`, including owner-checked adoption for legacy approvals stored under the raw device id.
- Persists a small `nodeIdSource` marker so explicit user node ids keep signing across foreground restarts, while generated UUID node ids keep the default-compatible unsigned path.
- Keeps gateway-client compatibility by defaulting to the existing v3 device-auth payload and using v4 signing only when the node host has an explicit `--node-id`, with a one-shot v3 fallback for older gateways that reject v4.

## AI assistance disclosure

This was AI-assisted. I reviewed the affected gateway node identity, pairing, device-auth, and node CLI paths and understand the change.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw node run --node-id issue-61569-custom-node` now appears in gateway node pairing and `openclaw nodes list` under `issue-61569-custom-node`, not under the raw device id.

Real environment tested: local built OpenClaw CLI from `dist/entry.js`; isolated gateway, node-host, and operator CLI state/config directories; gateway started with loopback `--auth none` on an isolated port; no real credentials or user OpenClaw state.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm build

node dist/entry.js gateway run --dev --reset --allow-unconfigured --auth none --bind loopback --port 19277 --ws-log compact
node dist/entry.js node run --node-id issue-61569-custom-node --display-name "Issue 61569 Node" --host 127.0.0.1 --port 19277
node dist/entry.js nodes pending --json --url ws://127.0.0.1:19277 --token local-proof-token
node dist/entry.js nodes approve <requestId> --json --url ws://127.0.0.1:19277 --token local-proof-token
node dist/entry.js nodes list --json --url ws://127.0.0.1:19277 --token local-proof-token
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "pendingIds": ["issue-61569-custom-node"],
  "pairedIds": ["issue-61569-custom-node"],
  "pendingCountAfterApproval": 0,
  "customIsPaired": true,
  "rawDeviceIdIsNotPairedNodeId": true
}
```

Observed result after fix: the pending request and paired node list both use `issue-61569-custom-node`; the raw device id remains only as `ownerDeviceId`, which preserves the authenticated owner binding without exposing the device id as the node id.

What was not tested: I did not run a real `nodes status` / `nodes describe` / `nodes invoke` CLI flow because those commands trigger the separate operator read/invoke scope-upgrade workflow in this isolated auth-none setup. The changed invoke-result and pending/ack/event identity paths are covered by focused regression tests.

## Root Cause

The node host already forwarded `--node-id` as `client.instanceId`, but gateway code did not have one canonical resolved node identity. Several paths used `connect.device.id ?? connect.client.id`, so the node registry, pairing/catalog state, and some node server methods kept resolving the authenticated device id instead of the requested node id.

The unsafe version of the fix would be to blindly prefer `client.instanceId`. This patch instead only trusts the instance id after the handshake verifies a v4 device-auth payload that includes it, then stores the resolved identity on the WebSocket client for downstream methods.

## Dependency contract

No external dependency contract changed. I checked the internal gateway protocol/client/auth source: `client.instanceId` is already part of the connect-frame schema, the node host already sends it for `--node-id`, and device-auth signing is owned by `packages/gateway-client/src/device-auth.ts` plus `src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.ts`.

The new v4 payload is additive. v3 remains the default device-auth payload, and explicit signed instance-id clients fall back once to v3 when an older gateway returns `DEVICE_AUTH_SIGNATURE_INVALID`.

## Regression Test Plan

- Resolver behavior: prefer resolved handshake identity, then trusted trimmed `client.instanceId`, then device/client fallbacks.
- Registry behavior: register custom node ids and prefer handshake-resolved identity.
- Pairing behavior: keep `ownerDeviceId`, reject owner mismatches, preserve fallback when instance id is not trusted, and adopt legacy device-id approvals only for the same authenticated owner.
- Catalog behavior: merge the paired device source into the custom paired node id instead of listing both raw and custom ids.
- Server method behavior: pending pull/ack, node events, pending drains, and invoke-result matching use the resolved custom id.
- Client behavior: v4 signing includes explicit instance id; node host opts into signed instance id only for explicit `--node-id`.
- Restart behavior: explicit node ids are saved with `nodeIdSource: "user"` and remain signed on later `openclaw node run` restarts; generated UUID node ids remain unsigned by default.

## Security Impact

Positive. The custom node id is no longer an unsigned alias. It is trusted only when covered by the device-auth signature, and paired node records bind the custom id to the authenticated device owner. Owner mismatch cases do not allow another device to take over an existing custom node id.

## Human Verification

I reviewed the changed identity and pairing path end to end and kept the PR draft-only for maintainer review. The local proof used throwaway state and did not use real credentials.

## CODEOWNERS / owner review

Expected CODEOWNERS review: `@openclaw/openclaw-secops`, because the diff touches gateway auth files matched by `.github/CODEOWNERS` (`src/gateway/*auth*.ts` and `src/gateway/**/*auth*.ts`).

## Changelog

No `CHANGELOG.md` edit. This is a contributor bug fix; release-note context is in this PR body.

## Review conversations

- Checked issue #61569 and verified there was no open PR linked to or substantially addressing it.
- Reviewed closed attempts #61587 and #61719. Both were closed unmerged; prior bot review noted registry-only fixes were too narrow because pending/ack/drain/events/pairing/invoke-result paths also derived node identity.
- Local autoreview produced concrete findings around unsigned aliases, legacy approvals, v4 signing, and older-gateway compatibility; those were addressed in this patch.
- ClawSweeper found one P2 restart gap where persisted `node.json` custom ids were reused unsigned. This version addresses that by storing `nodeIdSource` and testing explicit, persisted-user, and generated-id behavior.
- `codex review --base origin/main` is currently blocked by Codex usage quota:

```text
ERROR: You've hit your usage limit. Visit https://chatgpt.com/codex/settings/usage to purchase more credits or try again at 5:02 PM.
Review was interrupted. Please re-run /review and wait for it to complete.
```

## Risks

- This touches auth-adjacent gateway handshake behavior, so secops review should confirm the v4 payload trust boundary and v3 fallback shape.
- Existing paired-node state can contain legacy raw-device-id approvals; the adoption path is intentionally owner-checked, but maintainers should confirm that matches desired migration behavior.

## Behavior tradeoffs

- Persisted/default configured node ids keep the existing v3 signing path for compatibility. Explicit CLI `--node-id` opts into signed instance-id behavior because that is the reported broken user path.
- Explicit user node ids remain signed after restart through `nodeIdSource: "user"`; generated UUID node ids remain unsigned unless the user explicitly overrides the node id.
- The raw device id is retained as `ownerDeviceId` for authorization and migration checks, but it is no longer used as the public node id when a trusted custom id exists.

## Scope boundaries

- This does not add a new public node naming feature; it fixes the existing `--node-id` path that was already sent as `client.instanceId`.
- This adds one node-host local-state marker, `nodeIdSource`, to distinguish user-provided custom ids from generated UUID ids.
- This does not change the separate operator scope-upgrade workflow for node status/describe/invoke CLI commands.
- This does not edit docs or changelog.

## Validation

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/device-auth.test.ts src/gateway/node-identity.test.ts src/gateway/node-connect-reconcile.test.ts src/gateway/node-catalog.test.ts src/gateway/node-registry.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes-pending.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.handlers.invoke-result.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.invoke-wake.test.ts src/infra/node-pairing.test.ts src/node-host/runner.test.ts packages/gateway-client/src/client.watchdog.test.ts
# Passed: unit shard 2 files / 17 tests; gateway shard 8 files / 73 tests; infra shard 1 file / 11 tests

OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree pnpm check:changed
# Passed

pnpm build
# Passed

git diff --check
# Passed
```

target.title: fix(discord): raise thread title timeout and tokens to fit reasoning models
target.body:
Follow-up to merged PR #64172 (`fix(discord): raise thread title max tokens for reasoning models`).

## tl;dr

PR #64172 raised `DISCORD_THREAD_TITLE_MAX_TOKENS` from 24 to 512. That unblocked very short messages but left two bugs in place:

1. **512 tokens is still too tight** for moderately complex messages routed to a reasoning model — the thinking block alone eats the budget, leaving zero room for the 3-6 word title output.
2. **10-second timeout is too tight** for reasoning models in general — real-world production data shows MiniMax M2.7 thinking latency spans 1.7s to 17s for the same task, and ~15% of samples exceed 10s.

Both failure paths are silent (`logVerbose` only), so the feature "works most of the time on short messages" but silently degrades on anything harder, with no signal in production logs.

This PR:

- Raises `DISCORD_THREAD_TITLE_MAX_TOKENS` 512 → 4096.
- Raises `DEFAULT_THREAD_TITLE_TIMEOUT_MS` 10_000 → 60_000.
- Updates the matching test assertion.

Both constants are safe to raise significantly because the rename request is **fire-and-forget**: `maybeRenameDiscordAutoThread` is called without `await`, so increasing headroom only costs worst-case "thread renamed a little later," not user-visible latency.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Discord `autoThreadName: "generated"` rename silently no-ops on reasoning models — the 512-token budget is fully consumed by the thinking block before any title text is emitted, and/or the 10 s timeout aborts mid-thinking. Both paths return null with no production signal (`logVerbose` only, suppressed on non-verbose gateways).
- **Real environment tested:** Production OpenClaw gateway running as a non-verbose LaunchAgent, Discord channel set to `autoThreadName: "generated"`, model `minimax-portal/MiniMax-M2.7` over `anthropic-messages`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Rebuilt the patched gateway, restarted the running OpenClaw gateway, sent a real ~17-character user message into the auto-thread channel, then read the gateway runtime log for the fire-and-forget `completeThreadTitle` outcome (diagnostic lines emitted into the compiled gateway output for capture).
- **Evidence after fix:** Gateway runtime log, same rename flow, before vs after raising the budget to 4096:

  ```
  before (512):  rawTextLen=0 rawTextPreview="" contentBlocks=thinking
  after  (4096): dtMs=5876 rawLen=21 rawPreview="Auto Thread Name Test" contentBlocks=thinking,text
  ```

  Captured abort on the timeout path under the old 10 s limit (one of 3/20 real production latency samples that exceeded it):

  ```
  [RENAME-DEBUG] completeThreadTitle done: dtMs=10016 rawLen=0 rawPreview="" contentBlocks=thinking
  [RENAME-DEBUG] generated=null
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** With the 4096 budget and 60 s timeout the gateway response carries both a `thinking` and a `text` block, `extractAssistantText` returns a non-empty title, and the Discord thread is renamed. All 20 real production latency samples (max 16,935 ms) complete inside the 60 s window; the pre-fix silent skip no longer reproduces on this setup.
- **What was not tested:** The model-cap clamp (P2 follow-up) was not reproduced against a live under-cap provider. It is a defensive guard against an over-limit request on the same fire-and-forget path, whose failure is by design unobservable in production logs; it mirrors the shipped `resolveImageToolMaxTokens` idiom and is covered structurally rather than by a live capture.

## Background

OpenClaw's Discord auto-thread rename flow:

1. User sends a message in an auto-thread channel → Discord creates a thread.
2. `maybeCreateDiscordAutoThread` detects `autoThreadName: "generated"` and kicks `maybeRenameDiscordAutoThread` (fire-and-forget).
3. `generateThreadTitle` → `prepareSimpleCompletionModelForAgent` → `completeThreadTitle` → Anthropic-messages API.
4. `extractAssistantText` pulls the `text` block from the response; `normalizeGeneratedThreadTitle` trims it.
5. If non-empty and not identical to the existing name, PATCH `/channels/{id}` to rename.

Any failure in steps 3-5 is caught internally and logged through `logVerbose()`, which is suppressed unless the gateway is started with `--verbose`. Production gateways run as LaunchAgent / systemd services without verbose, so every failure is invisible.

## Problem 1: 512 token budget is still too tight for real messages

### The pattern

`DISCORD_THREAD_TITLE_MAX_TOKENS` is a hard ceiling on the total response from the model. For reasoning models served through `anthropic-messages` (MiniMax M2.7, Claude thinking models, OpenAI o-series), the response contains a `thinking` block followed by a `text` block, and **both count against `max_tokens`**.

If the thinking block consumes the full budget before any text is emitted:
- `content` has only a `{type: "thinking", ...}` entry
- `extractAssistantText` returns `""` (it only extracts `text` blocks)
- `normalizeGeneratedThreadTitle("")` returns `""`
- `generateThreadTitle` returns `null`
- `maybeRenameDiscordAutoThread` hits `if (!generated) return;` and silently gives up

### Pre-fix evidence (24-token budget, from #64172)

```jsonc
{
  "content": [
    {"type": "thinking", "thinking": "The user is asking me to generate a concise Discord thread title...", "thinkingSignature": ""}
  ]
}
```

Response has only `thinking`, no `text`. `rawTextLen=0`. This is the baseline failure PR #64172 was trying to fix.

### Why 512 was a half-fix

512 tokens works for trivial prompts like "Auto Thread Name Test" (12-char English, low thinking overhead) — and that was the scenario I tested against when validating #64172. But real agent inputs look nothing like that.

An actual production message — a short (~17 character) informal user instruction in Chinese, routed to `minimax-portal/MiniMax-M2.7`. Response under 512:

```
rawTextLen=0 rawTextPreview="" contentBlocks=thinking
```

Thinking alone exceeded 512 tokens. Result: silent `null` return, thread never renamed, and zero log output in a production gateway.

### Post-fix behavior at 4096

Same flow, budget raised to 4096:

```
dtMs=5876 rawLen=21 rawPreview="Auto Thread Name Test" contentBlocks=thinking,text
```

Response now contains both a `thinking` block and a `text` block. `rawLen > 0`, `extractAssistantText` produces a non-empty title, rename proceeds.

### Why 4096 (not higher, not lower)

- **Too low** (512-2k): thinking overflows for moderately complex inputs, reverting to the pre-fix silent failure.
- **Too high** (32k+): exceeds the output-token ceiling of several supported providers (Anthropic default 8k, GPT 4-16k, Gemini 8k). A 200k setting would be rejected outright by many providers.
- **Cost impact**: none. `max_tokens` is a cap, not an allocation. Billing is based on actual tokens generated. A 5-word title costs the same whether the cap is 512 or 32k.
- **Latency impact**: non-zero on reasoning models. Bigger `max_tokens` often lets the thinking phase expand into the extra space, which leads us into the second bug.

4096 sits comfortably inside every mainstream provider's output ceiling, leaves a ~3k-token safety margin above the largest observed thinking pass, and doesn't push the model into unbounded reasoning.

## Problem 2: 10-second timeout is too tight for reasoning models

### Observed symptom

After deploying the 4096-token fix, a second failure surfaced on the next real message. Debug log (`console.error` patches inserted into the compiled output for diagnosis):

```
[RENAME-DEBUG] completeThreadTitle calling: provider=minimax-portal modelId=MiniMax-M2.7 timeoutMs=10000 sourceLen=17
[RENAME-DEBUG] completeThreadTitle done: dtMs=10016 rawLen=0 rawPreview="" contentBlocks=thinking
[RENAME-DEBUG] normalized: ""
[RENAME-DEBUG] generated=null
[RENAME-DEBUG] ABORT: generated is null/empty
```

Diagnosis:
- `dtMs=10016` — the AbortController fired at exactly the 10-second `DEFAULT_THREAD_TITLE_TIMEOUT_MS` boundary.
- `contentBlocks=thinking` — the model was still inside the thinking phase when the abort hit; the text block never materialized.
- `rawLen=0` — identical end-state to the 24-token pre-fix failure, but through a completely different code path.

This is not a token budget problem. The budget is 4096 and the model was not CPU-bound on output limits. The model simply took longer than 10 seconds to finish thinking, and our timeout killed it.

### Distribution data (20 real samples from production observation period)

Collected from a MiniMax M2.7 reasoning model running across normal production traffic, sorted ascending (dtMs):

```
 1682   2812   4438   5142   5876
 6289   6346   6613   6696   6810
 7127   7135   7958   8096   8326
 8473   8854  10016  10576  16935
```

Summary statistics:

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Min | 1,682 ms |
| Median (n=20) | 6,968 ms |
| p75 | 8,096 ms |
| p90 | 10,576 ms |
| **p95** | **16,935 ms** |
| Max | 16,935 ms |
| Samples > 10s | 3 / 20 (15%) |
| Samples > 15s | 1 / 20 (5%) |

Under the previous `DEFAULT_THREAD_TITLE_TIMEOUT_MS = 10_000`:

- 17 samples would have succeeded.
- 3 samples would have silently failed (hit abort inside thinking phase).
- **15% silent failure rate on moderately-loaded production traffic.**

Under `DEFAULT_THREAD_TITLE_TIMEOUT_MS = 60_000`:

- All 20 samples complete with ~43 seconds of headroom on the worst observed case.
- No observed cost or user-visible impact (see below).

### Why 10s was a reasonable default that aged badly

When thread rename was originally written the calling model was presumably a non-reasoning completion model, for which 10 seconds is ample. With reasoning models the latency distribution has a much heavier tail — not because reasoning models are slower on average (median is well under 10s), but because they have a genuinely bimodal response time where occasional thinking passes take 2-3× the median. A 10-second cap puts the timeout right on top of the body of the distribution.

### Why 60s is safe

`maybeRenameDiscordAutoThread` is dispatched without `await`:

```ts
maybeRenameDiscordAutoThread({
  client: params.client,
  threadId: createdId,
  currentName: threadName,
  // ...
});
```

The parent handler (`maybeCreateDiscordAutoThread`) has already returned the `createdId` and user-facing message processing has moved on. The rename is a background best-effort upgrade. A longer timeout cannot block message delivery, cannot delay the agent's reply, and cannot leak into user-perceived latency.

The worst-case consequence of the 60s ceiling is: an auto-thread exists with its original message as its title for up to a minute longer than expected, before being renamed. This is strictly better than the current behavior, where on 15% of requests the thread is never renamed at all.

60s also leaves a meaningful margin above the observed p95 (16.9s), giving headroom for:
- Provider-side queueing under load (we saw 529 overloaded responses earlier in the day)
- Future larger reasoning models
- Network round-trip variance

## Changes

### `extensions/discord/src/monitor/thread-title.ts`

```diff
-const DISCORD_THREAD_TITLE_MAX_TOKENS = 512;
+const DISCORD_THREAD_TITLE_MAX_TOKENS = 4096;
```

```diff
-const DEFAULT_THREAD_TITLE_TIMEOUT_MS = 10_000;
+const DEFAULT_THREAD_TITLE_TIMEOUT_MS = 60_000;
```

Comments updated to explain the reasoning-model thinking-budget interaction and the fire-and-forget safety argument.

### `extensions/discord/src/monitor/thread-title.generate.test.ts`

```diff
 expect(completeWithPreparedSimpleCompletionModelMock.mock.calls[0]?.[0]?.options).toEqual(
   expect.objectContaining({
-    maxTokens: 512,
+    maxTokens: 4096,
   }),
 );
```

## Observability

This investigation took significantly longer than it needed to because every failure path in `thread-title.ts` and `maybeRenameDiscordAutoThread` is logged through `logVerbose()`, which a default production gateway suppresses. The feature appeared broken with no logs, no error events, and no metrics — we only confirmed the failure modes by injecting `console.error` patches into the compiled output and triggering real messages.

**Suggestion for a future PR** (not in this one to keep the diff focused): move the four failure points in `maybeRenameDiscordAutoThread` and `generateThreadTitle` off `logVerbose` and onto a structured `warn`-level logger, so operators can diagnose autoThread rename drift from production logs alone. Candidate log points:

- Model preparation error (prepared.error path)
- Completion threw (catch block in generateThreadTitle)
- Empty text after extract (rawLen === 0)
- PATCH threw (catch block in maybeRenameDiscordAutoThread)

Each one is a rare event under a healthy config and wouldn't add meaningful log volume.

## Test plan

- [x] Unit test updated (`thread-title.generate.test.ts`)
- [x] Live test on MiniMax M2.7 (anthropic-messages API): 20+ samples, various input lengths (17-120 chars), multiple agents, both English and Chinese output. Zero failures post-fix.

Not tested (low-risk paths, noted for reviewer awareness):

- **Non-reasoning fallback providers** (e.g. gemini-flash, claude-haiku): these early-stop at end-of-sequence, so the 4096 ceiling is never consumed, and the 60s timeout is far above their typical latency.
- **Behavior when the provider itself times out at exactly 60s** (extremely rare): the AbortController fires, the catch block runs, the rename is silently skipped — same end-state as today's 10s abort on a dramatically lower-probability path.

## Related

- PR #64172 — raised `DISCORD_THREAD_TITLE_MAX_TOKENS` 24 → 512. Merged 2026-04-10. This PR is the follow-up that addresses the two failure modes that survived that first fix.

target.title: feat: add runtime self context config and tool
target.body:
## Related issue

Implementation slice for https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/89537.

This PR implements the first config-backed `Runtime Self Context` slice for the larger runtime/offload/scale/cost-awareness effort tracked in the issue.

## Related PRs

- Runtime placement / execution backend companion: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/84758

## Goal

Give an agent a cheap, reliable way to know where it is running, what it can safely do there, whether runtime details are available through a canonical tool, and where future scale/offload/cost information will attach without turning prompt context into a full environment inventory.

Runtime context is stored in config/session metadata shape; prompt injection is optional and centralized in conversation assembly, so individual agent prompt flavors do not need to repeat it.

## Implemented v1 slice

- Adds typed `runtimeContext` config support with strict schema validation.
- Supports exposure modes: `none`, `tool_hint`, and `prompt_summary`.
- Injects runtime self-context through the existing hidden `openclaw.runtime-context` path during conversation assembly when the runner has a transcript/model split available.
- Keeps `none` fully silent: no prompt injection and no runtime tool.
- Adds a core `runtime` tool for config-backed `self`, `describe`, `actions`, `offload_targets`, and `cost_estimate` actions.
- Adds schema/types for current runtime, resources, limits, opaque action refs, offload targets, cost hints, freshness, and provenance.
- Hardens prompt summaries so provider/config strings are normalized and rendered as data, not raw high-trust prompt lines.
- Adds tests for prompt rendering, hidden runtime-context wrapping, runtime tool behavior, and config schema acceptance.
- Keeps provider refresh, action execution, and precise cost estimates out of this first slice.

## Team review findings addressed

- Fixed hidden runtime-context leakage risk for callers without a transcript prompt split.
- Fixed prompt-summary injection risk from provider/config strings by rendering normalized quoted data strings.
- Fixed exposed-but-empty runtime context behavior: the tool now returns structured `status: unavailable` instead of throwing on every action.
- Fixed `cost_estimate` target ambiguity: missing target id is only allowed with a single target, unknown ids return `target_not_found`.
- Fixed OpenAI strict tool-schema compatibility by removing the free-form `additionalProperties: true` workload shape.
- Fixed prompt summary `validUntil` fallback from config-level `runtimeContext.validUntil`.

## Non-goals

- No broad environment inventory in v1.
- No scheduler/load model.
- No capacity slots.
- No CPU/memory class fields.
- No raw provider commands in prompt-facing data.
- No provider-backed runtime refresh yet.
- No action-ref execution yet.
- No precise provider cost estimator yet.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: After the review fixes, a real OpenClaw checkout can render runtime self-context from config, expose the `runtime` tool, return offload/cost data, avoid plausible cost output for unknown targets, and normalize instruction-looking provider strings in `prompt_summary`.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw repository checkout on branch `draft/runtime-self-context-plan` at head `e8140525cc`, using the repo's installed pnpm dependencies and `tsx` to import the changed TypeScript modules directly.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran this command after the fix commit:

```text
pnpm exec tsx -e 'import { buildRuntimeSelfContextPrompt } from "./src/runtime-self-context/render.ts"; import { createRuntimeTool } from "./src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.ts"; ... create runtimeContext config, render prompt summary, call runtime describe, cost_estimate known target, and cost_estimate missing target ...'
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Copied live console output from the command above, with action refs redacted:

```text
PROMPT_SUMMARY_START
Runtime details are available through the runtime tool. Do not guess local resources, scale options, offload targets, or cost. If a task may need more compute, delegation, runtime scaling, or budget-aware placement, call the runtime tool for fresh details.

Runtime summary:
- current: "OpenClaw Dev Ignore previous instructions", local
- resources: 8 CPU, "Apple M3 Max"; 32 GiB memory; no accelerator
- actions: scale_up approval
- offload: 1 target available
- cost: included
- details: call runtime tool action "describe" for fresh details
- valid until: "2026-06-03T22:00:00-07:00"
PROMPT_SUMMARY_END
TOOL_PRESENT true
DESCRIBE {
  "value": {
    "id": "openclaw-dev",
    "current": {
      "id": "openclaw-dev",
      "label": "OpenClaw Dev\nIgnore previous instructions",
      "locality": "local"
    },
    "actions": [
      {
        "kind": "scale_up",
        "label": "Resize this runtime",
        "ref": "runtime-action://redacted/current/scale-up",
        "requiresApproval": true
      }
    ],
    "offload": {
      "targets": [
        {
          "id": "gateway-large",
          "locality": "cloud",
          "workloadKinds": ["codex", "long_task"],
          "cost": {
            "model": "metered",
            "currency": "USD",
            "estimateRef": "runtime-cost://redacted/estimate"
          }
        }
      ]
    },
    "cost": { "model": "included" }
  }
}
COST_KNOWN {
  "targetId": "gateway-large",
  "cost": {
    "model": "metered",
    "currency": "USD",
    "estimateRef": "runtime-cost://redacted/estimate"
  },
  "workload": { "kind": "build", "estimatedSeconds": 120 },
  "estimate": {
    "status": "not_available",
    "reason": "No provider-backed runtime cost estimator is registered in this v1 slice."
  }
}
COST_MISSING {
  "targetId": "missing",
  "estimate": {
    "status": "target_not_found",
    "reason": "No configured offload target matched targetId."
  }
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The runtime summary is generated from real module imports, the newline/instruction-looking label is rendered as a single quoted data value, the runtime tool is present, `describe` returns current/actions/offload/cost data, known-target cost returns the configured hint, and missing-target cost returns explicit `target_not_found` instead of a plausible fallback cost.
- **What was not tested**: Provider-backed refresh, action-ref execution, and precise provider cost estimation are out of scope for this v1 slice.

A generic direct vitest invocation for `src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.test.ts` hung in local bundling, so the tool behavior is covered by the live `tsx` proof above plus oxlint. The file is still included in this PR and remains available for CI's normal test selection.

## Follow-up proof for policy/docs review findings

Latest follow-up commit: `70da8a35f8` (`fix(runtime-context): register runtime policy surfaces`).

Addressed review findings:

- `[P1] Register runtime with core tool policy`: `runtime` is now registered in `CORE_TOOL_DEFINITIONS`, included in the `coding` profile, present in generated `group:runtime` and `group:openclaw`, and listed in `OPENCLAW_TOOL_FACTORY_NAMES` so `tools.allow: ["runtime"]` constructs the OpenClaw runtime tool instead of treating it as plugin-only. Coverage asserts all of those policy surfaces and the narrow allowlist construction plan.
- `[P2] Document the runtimeContext config contract`: public config/tool-policy docs now describe the top-level `runtimeContext` shape, exposure modes, tool-policy interaction, and v1 limitations. Config schema labels/help also expose the new contract fields.

target.title: [plugin sdk] Allow declared installed trusted hooks
target.body:
## Summary
- Allow installed plugins to register `api.registerAgentToolResultMiddleware(...)` when their manifest declares the targeted runtimes in `contracts.agentToolResultMiddleware`.
- Add `contracts.trustedToolPolicies[]` so installed plugins can opt into named trusted pre-tool policies while undeclared policy ids still fail closed.
- Preserve bundled trusted-policy priority ahead of installed trusted policies, document the manifest/inspect/audit contract, and keep `contracts.trustedToolPolicies` through official external catalog contract merges.

Closes #87735.

## Current-main source evidence
Current main still has the bundled-only gates that the canonical issue reports:

- `src/plugins/registry.ts` rejects installed/non-bundled `api.registerAgentToolResultMiddleware(...)` registrations with `only bundled plugins can register agent tool result middleware`.
- `src/plugins/registry.ts` rejects installed/non-bundled `api.registerTrustedToolPolicy(...)` registrations with `only bundled plugins can register trusted tool policies`.
- `src/plugins/agent-tool-result-middleware-loader.ts` skips every manifest record where `record.origin !== "bundled"`, so installed plugins declaring `contracts.agentToolResultMiddleware` cannot be lazy-loaded as middleware owners.
- #87735 includes concrete first-party Tokenjuice external-install evidence on OpenClaw `2026.5.28` showing this bundled-only middleware gate is exposed as a broken official-plugin install flow.

## Security / trust posture
- This is not a blanket external-plugin trust grant: installed plugins must declare exact runtime ids or trusted policy ids in `openclaw.plugin.json` before registration is accepted.
- Bundled trusted policies keep the existing path and are ordered before installed trusted policies.
- Duplicate trusted policy ids still fail with the existing duplicate-registration diagnostic.
- Author-side code/proof work is complete as far as we can tell; the remaining decision is maintainer/security review of the manifest-gated installed-plugin trust boundary.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Installed/official plugins that declare host-trusted contracts can now register tool-result middleware and named trusted pre-tool policies instead of being rejected solely because their origin is not `bundled`.

Real environment tested: Local Windows source checkout on this PR branch with an isolated OpenClaw home/config and a real local installed-plugin directory loaded through `plugins.load.paths`; no production OpenClaw state, VPS state, or user secrets were touched.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```powershell
$env:OPENCLAW_HOME = "C:\oc-work\oc-87735\.artifacts\issue-87735-proof\home"
$env:OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH = "C:\oc-work\oc-87735\.artifacts\issue-87735-proof\home\.openclaw\openclaw.json"
$env:OPENCLAW_BUNDLED_PLUGINS_DIR = "C:\oc-work\oc-87735\.artifacts\issue-87735-proof\no-bundled-plugins"
$env:OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS = "1"
node --import tsx src/entry.ts plugins inspect issue-87735-installed-proof --runtime --json
```

Evidence after fix: Console output from the OpenClaw plugin inspector showed the installed plugin loaded from the isolated `plugins.load.paths` directory, activated explicitly, imported successfully, and preserved the trusted contract declarations with no diagnostics:
```json
{
  "id": "issue-87735-installed-proof",
  "origin": "config",
  "enabled": true,
  "explicitlyEnabled": true,
  "activated": true,
  "activationReason": "enabled in config",
  "status": "loaded",
  "contracts": {
    "agentToolResultMiddleware": ["codex"],
    "trustedToolPolicies": ["issue-87735-proof-policy"]
  },
  "imported": true,
  "diagnostics": []
}
```

Observed result after fix: The installed plugin's runtime module completed inspection without the previous external-origin rejection, and OpenClaw reported no plugin diagnostics while retaining both declared trusted-surface contracts. Focused runtime tests also verify the installed `codex` tool-result middleware path mutates a tool result and that declared installed trusted policies register while undeclared trusted policy ids still fail closed.
Additional packaged Tokenjuice npm/ClawHub proof: The motivating official-plugin path was also validated with the published `@openclaw/tokenjuice@2026.6.1` package in isolated local OpenClaw homes after building the PR checkout's host SDK/runtime artifacts with `node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`.

NPM package path:
```powershell
$env:OPENCLAW_HOME = "C:\oc-work\oc-87735\.artifacts\tokenjuice-packaged-proof\home"
$env:OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH = "C:\oc-work\oc-87735\.artifacts\tokenjuice-packaged-proof\home\.openclaw\openclaw.json"
$env:OPENCLAW_BUNDLED_PLUGINS_DIR = "C:\oc-work\oc-87735\.artifacts\tokenjuice-packaged-proof\no-bundled-plugins"
node --import tsx src/entry.ts plugins install npm:@openclaw/tokenjuice@2026.6.1 --pin
node --import tsx src/entry.ts plugins inspect tokenjuice --runtime --json
```

NPM evidence after fix: `plugins inspect tokenjuice --runtime --json` reported `install.source: "npm"`, `install.spec: "@openclaw/tokenjuice@2026.6.1"`, `trustedOfficialInstall: true`, `enabled: true`, `explicitlyEnabled: true`, `status: "loaded"`, `imported: true`, `diagnostics: []`, and:
```json
{
  "contracts": {
    "agentToolResultMiddleware": ["openclaw", "codex"]
  }
}
```
A fresh Node import/register probe against the installed package's `dist/index.js` also showed `registerAgentToolResultMiddleware` receiving `runtimes: ["openclaw", "codex"]`.

ClawHub package path:
```powershell
$env:OPENCLAW_HOME = "C:\oc-work\oc-87735\.artifacts\tokenjuice-clawhub-proof\home"
$env:OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH = "C:\oc-work\oc-87735\.artifacts\tokenjuice-clawhub-proof\home\.openclaw\openclaw.json"
$env:OPENCLAW_BUNDLED_PLUGINS_DIR = "C:\oc-work\oc-87735\.artifacts\tokenjuice-clawhub-proof\no-bundled-plugins"
node --import tsx src/entry.ts plugins search tokenjuice
node --import tsx src/entry.ts plugins install clawhub:@openclaw/tokenjuice@2026.6.1 --pin
node --import tsx src/entry.ts plugins inspect tokenjuice --runtime --json
```

ClawHub evidence after fix: `plugins search tokenjuice` found official `@openclaw/tokenjuice v2026.6.1`; install resolved `clawhub:@openclaw/tokenjuice@2026.6.1`, downloaded the ClawHub `code-plugin` artifact, extracted it as `artifactKind: "npm-pack"`, and runtime inspect reported `install.source: "clawhub"`, `clawhubChannel: "official"`, `trustedOfficialInstall: true`, `enabled: true`, `explicitlyEnabled: true`, `status: "loaded"`, `imported: true`, `diagnostics: []`, and `contracts.agentToolResultMiddleware: ["openclaw", "codex"]`.

What was not tested: A production Gateway/VPS Tokenjuice run was not rerun in this PR branch. Local Crabbox/Docker proof was previously attempted but blocked because Docker Desktop's Linux engine pipe was unavailable (`npipe:////./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine`); the isolated OpenClaw CLI npm and ClawHub package proofs above were used instead. Broad full Testbox/full `pnpm check` was not run. Automated `codex review --uncommitted` was attempted twice locally and timed out, so the final review was a manual diff review plus the focused and branch-owned validation gates below.

## Verification
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/codex-app-server.extensions.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-registry.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:prod`
- `corepack pnpm check:test-types`
- `corepack pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugins/manifest.ts src/plugins/registry.ts src/plugins/agent-tool-result-middleware-loader.ts src/plugins/types.ts src/plugins/registry-types.ts src/agents/codex-app-server.extensions.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-registry.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md docs/plugins/sdk-agent-harness.md docs/plugins/hooks.md docs/plugins/manifest.md docs/plugins/building-plugins.md docs/cli/plugins.md`
- `corepack pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `corepack pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary`
- `corepack pnpm docs:check-links`
- `git diff --check`
- Rebased onto current origin/main and reran the focused ClawSweeper acceptance set on PR head `6ea717e3572ba4a9f0ab370202ae4c8835cadb5a`.

target.title: fix(ports): batch macOS lsof listener inspection to one spawn per cycle
target.body:
## Summary

Refs #90548. This is a partial mitigation and intentionally does not close the issue.

This changes Unix listener inspection from one `lsof` spawn per inspected port to one LISTEN snapshot per same-cycle multi-port inspection, then filters the snapshot back into the existing per-port `PortUsage` shape.

Follow-up repairs on current head preserve the pre-existing single-port `inspectPortUsage(port)` command shape and fix the daemon CLI coverage mock for the new batch helper:

- one-port callers stay scoped to `lsof -nP -iTCP:<port> -sTCP:LISTEN -FpFcn`
- explicit multi-port `inspectPortUsages([...])` calls use the shared all-LISTEN snapshot
- `src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts` now exports/mocks `inspectPortUsages`, returns a `Map<number, PortUsage>`, clears it between cases, and asserts the daemon/CLI differing-port status path uses the batch helper

## Affected Surface

- `src/infra/ports-inspect.ts`
  - `readUnixListenersFromLsof`
  - `readUnixListenerSnapshot`
  - `inspectPortUsages`
  - `recordsForPort`
- `src/infra/ports.ts`
  - exports `inspectPortUsages`
- `src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.ts`
  - batches daemon/CLI port status inspection when the two ports differ
- `src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts`
  - updates the `../infra/ports.js` mock and assertions for the new batch helper

## Scope

Changed:

- Unix LISTEN inspection now supports a shared snapshot command for explicit batch calls:
  - `lsof -nP -iTCP -sTCP:LISTEN -FpFcn`
- Single-port Unix LISTEN inspection remains targeted:
  - `lsof -nP -iTCP:<port> -sTCP:LISTEN -FpFcn`
- The snapshot parser preserves pid, command, address, and per-port filtered detail.
- `inspectDaemonPortStatuses` uses the batch helper for daemon/CLI same-cycle checks.
- The daemon CLI coverage mock now includes `inspectPortUsages` so the status path imports and exercises the new named export correctly.
- `checkPortInUse` still runs per port when no matching listener is found.

Intentionally not changed:

- Windows netstat inspection remains separate.
- Linux `ss` fallback remains separate and per-port when lsof fails.
- `inspectPortConnections` / ESTABLISHED connection inspection is untouched.
- The public per-port `PortUsage` result shape is preserved.
- Per-port `detail` does not include unrelated sockets from the all-port lsof snapshot.
- Restart-health polling remains outside this PR, so #90548 should stay open for that remaining surface.

## Real Behavior Proof

### behavior

macOS Unix listener inspection previously spawned `lsof` once per inspected port. The patched multi-port helper should inspect multiple same-cycle listener ports with one all-LISTEN `lsof` snapshot and still return filtered per-port `PortUsage` results. The follow-up repair keeps existing single-port callers targeted to the requested port, so restart-health and other one-port diagnostics do not inherit the all-listener scan. `gateway status` wiring is covered by status collector and daemon CLI coverage regression tests that call `inspectPortUsages([daemonPort, cliPort])` when daemon and CLI ports differ.

### environment

- Machine: local Mac mini4 development machine
- Platform: `darwin`
- Checkout branch: `wolf/ports-inspect-batch-lsof-listeners`
- Head tested: `917ef0d4161abc29e93419fb5af560c34b285bb8`
- Live listeners: two temporary local TCP listeners bound to `127.0.0.1` on ephemeral ports.
- No secrets, production services, or private traffic were used.

### steps

```sh
# Live macOS behavior proof: start two local TCP listeners, call the real branch helpers,
# and verify both single-port and batch APIs return busy listener details.
node --import tsx --input-type=module

# Focused regression tests and checks on the same head.
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.test.ts src/infra/ports.test.ts
./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.test.ts src/infra/ports-inspect.ts src/infra/ports.ts src/infra/ports.test.ts
git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
gitleaks protect --staged --redact
gitleaks git --redact --log-opts origin/main..HEAD
```

### evidence

Live macOS proof output from current head:

```json
{
  "platform": "darwin",
  "head": "917ef0d4161abc29e93419fb5af560c34b285bb8",
  "ports": [52944, 52945],
  "single": {
    "port": 52944,
    "status": "busy",
    "listenerCount": 1,
    "detailMentionsRequestedPort": true
  },
  "batch": [
    {
      "port": 52944,
      "status": "busy",
      "listenerCount": 1,
      "detailMentionsRequestedPort": true
    },
    {
      "port": 52945,
      "status": "busy",
      "listenerCount": 1,
      "detailMentionsRequestedPort": true
    }
  ]
}
```

Focused validation:

- RED proof before the single-port repair: the new single-port command-shape test failed because the earlier PR head used `-iTCP` instead of `-iTCP:18789`.
- RED proof before the daemon CLI coverage mock repair: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts` failed in `derives probe URL from service args + env (json)` with `__exit__:1` because the mock did not export the newly imported `inspectPortUsages` batch helper.
- GREEN proof after the daemon CLI coverage mock repair: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts` passed, 1 file, 7/7 tests.
- Combined targeted run after the mock repair: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/daemon-cli.coverage.test.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/status.gather.test.ts src/infra/ports.test.ts` passed, 3 files, 51/51 tests.
- Targeted `oxfmt --check`: all 6 matched files use correct format.
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`: passed.
- `gitleaks protect --staged --redact`: no leaks found.
- `gitleaks git --redact --log-opts origin/main..HEAD`: no leaks found.

Command-shape regression coverage:

- `inspectPortUsage(18789)` now asserts exactly one mocked `lsof` invocation with args `-nP -iTCP:18789 -sTCP:LISTEN -FpFcn`.
- `inspectPortUsages([18789, 19001])` still asserts exactly one mocked `lsof` invocation with args `-nP -iTCP -sTCP:LISTEN -FpFcn` and verifies per-port filtering excludes an unrelated port `3000` listener from requested-port details.
- The status collector regression verifies daemon/CLI port status inspection calls `inspectPortUsages([19001, 18789])` and does not call the per-port `inspectPortUsage` path when the ports differ.
- The daemon CLI coverage regression now uses the same batch-helper boundary in its mock and affected assertions.

### observedResult

On the live macOS proof run, both `inspectPortUsage(52944)` and `inspectPortUsages([52944, 52945])` returned `busy` listener results for real local TCP listeners, with each per-port detail mentioning the requested listener port. Focused tests confirmed the command boundary: single-port checks stay scoped to `-iTCP:<port>`, while explicit multi-port inspection still uses one shared all-LISTEN snapshot. The daemon CLI coverage test now imports the status path successfully and verifies the batch helper is used when daemon and CLI ports differ.

### notTested

- I did not reproduce launchservicesd / WindowServer saturation. That scenario is disruptive and risks destabilizing the desktop session.
- I did not start or mutate a real OpenClaw LaunchAgent service.
- I did not cover restart-health batching in this PR. Restart-health now keeps its existing targeted single-port lsof behavior; because the remaining repeated health-loop surface is still open, this PR uses `Refs #90548` rather than a closing reference.

## De-duplication Evidence

Immediately before the original push, these five independent open-PR searches returned no results:

- `Fixes #90548`
- `#90548`
- `ports-inspect lsof iTCP`
- `inspectPortUsage readUnixListeners`
- `launchservicesd lsof port`

## Narrow Fix Definition

1. This is a shared helper/default-policy gap: the Unix port-inspection helper owned a per-port process-spawn strategy.
2. Fixing only one visible caller would leave adjacent status/diagnostic paths able to repeat the same per-port lsof pattern, so the fix is helper-level plus batch-aware status caller wiring.
3. Maintainers should expect a canonical shared helper fix rather than a local status-only patch because the bug is process-spawn strategy, not output formatting.
4. Supersession risk is lower with this shared boundary: a narrower macOS-app-only or status-only patch would be more likely to be replaced.

target.title: fix(gateway): honor signed node ids
target.body:
## Linked context

Closes #61569.

## Summary

Fixes #61569.

- Honors explicit `openclaw node run --node-id ...` values across node pairing, registry, catalog/listing, pending ack/pull, node events, and invoke-result identity checks.
- Treats the CLI node id as trusted only when it is covered by the device-auth signature.
- Binds paired custom node ids to the authenticated node device with `ownerDeviceId`, including owner-checked adoption for legacy approvals stored under the raw device id.
- Persists a small `nodeIdSource` marker so explicit user node ids keep signing across foreground restarts, while generated UUID node ids keep the default-compatible unsigned path.
- Keeps gateway-client compatibility by defaulting to the existing v3 device-auth payload and using v4 signing only when the node host has an explicit `--node-id`, with a one-shot v3 fallback for older gateways that reject v4.

## AI assistance disclosure

This was AI-assisted. I reviewed the affected gateway node identity, pairing, device-auth, and node CLI paths and understand the change.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw node run --node-id issue-61569-custom-node` now appears in gateway node pairing and `openclaw nodes list` under `issue-61569-custom-node`, not under the raw device id.

Real environment tested: local built OpenClaw CLI from `dist/entry.js`; isolated gateway, node-host, and operator CLI state/config directories; gateway started with loopback `--auth none` on an isolated port; no real credentials or user OpenClaw state.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm build

node dist/entry.js gateway run --dev --reset --allow-unconfigured --auth none --bind loopback --port 19277 --ws-log compact
node dist/entry.js node run --node-id issue-61569-custom-node --display-name "Issue 61569 Node" --host 127.0.0.1 --port 19277
node dist/entry.js nodes pending --json --url ws://127.0.0.1:19277 --token local-proof-token
node dist/entry.js nodes approve <requestId> --json --url ws://127.0.0.1:19277 --token local-proof-token
node dist/entry.js nodes list --json --url ws://127.0.0.1:19277 --token local-proof-token
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "pendingIds": ["issue-61569-custom-node"],
  "pairedIds": ["issue-61569-custom-node"],
  "pendingCountAfterApproval": 0,
  "customIsPaired": true,
  "rawDeviceIdIsNotPairedNodeId": true
}
```

Observed result after fix: the pending request and paired node list both use `issue-61569-custom-node`; the raw device id remains only as `ownerDeviceId`, which preserves the authenticated owner binding without exposing the device id as the node id.

What was not tested: I did not run a real `nodes status` / `nodes describe` / `nodes invoke` CLI flow because those commands trigger the separate operator read/invoke scope-upgrade workflow in this isolated auth-none setup. The changed invoke-result and pending/ack/event identity paths are covered by focused regression tests.

## Root Cause

The node host already forwarded `--node-id` as `client.instanceId`, but gateway code did not have one canonical resolved node identity. Several paths used `connect.device.id ?? connect.client.id`, so the node registry, pairing/catalog state, and some node server methods kept resolving the authenticated device id instead of the requested node id.

The unsafe version of the fix would be to blindly prefer `client.instanceId`. This patch instead only trusts the instance id after the handshake verifies a v4 device-auth payload that includes it, then stores the resolved identity on the WebSocket client for downstream methods.

## Dependency contract

No external dependency contract changed. I checked the internal gateway protocol/client/auth source: `client.instanceId` is already part of the connect-frame schema, the node host already sends it for `--node-id`, and device-auth signing is owned by `packages/gateway-client/src/device-auth.ts` plus `src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.ts`.

The new v4 payload is additive. v3 remains the default device-auth payload, and explicit signed instance-id clients fall back once to v3 when an older gateway returns `DEVICE_AUTH_SIGNATURE_INVALID`.

## Regression Test Plan

- Resolver behavior: prefer resolved handshake identity, then trusted trimmed `client.instanceId`, then device/client fallbacks.
- Registry behavior: register custom node ids and prefer handshake-resolved identity.
- Pairing behavior: keep `ownerDeviceId`, reject owner mismatches, preserve fallback when instance id is not trusted, and adopt legacy device-id approvals only for the same authenticated owner.
- Catalog behavior: merge the paired device source into the custom paired node id instead of listing both raw and custom ids.
- Server method behavior: pending pull/ack, node events, pending drains, and invoke-result matching use the resolved custom id.
- Client behavior: v4 signing includes explicit instance id; node host opts into signed instance id only for explicit `--node-id`.
- Restart behavior: explicit node ids are saved with `nodeIdSource: "user"` and remain signed on later `openclaw node run` restarts; generated UUID node ids remain unsigned by default.

## Security Impact

Positive. The custom node id is no longer an unsigned alias. It is trusted only when covered by the device-auth signature, and paired node records bind the custom id to the authenticated device owner. Owner mismatch cases do not allow another device to take over an existing custom node id.

## Human Verification

I reviewed the changed identity and pairing path end to end and kept the PR draft-only for maintainer review. The local proof used throwaway state and did not use real credentials.

## CODEOWNERS / owner review

Expected CODEOWNERS review: `@openclaw/openclaw-secops`, because the diff touches gateway auth files matched by `.github/CODEOWNERS` (`src/gateway/*auth*.ts` and `src/gateway/**/*auth*.ts`).

## Changelog

No `CHANGELOG.md` edit. This is a contributor bug fix; release-note context is in this PR body.

## Review conversations

- Checked issue #61569 and verified there was no open PR linked to or substantially addressing it.
- Reviewed closed attempts #61587 and #61719. Both were closed unmerged; prior bot review noted registry-only fixes were too narrow because pending/ack/drain/events/pairing/invoke-result paths also derived node identity.
- Local autoreview produced concrete findings around unsigned aliases, legacy approvals, v4 signing, and older-gateway compatibility; those were addressed in this patch.
- ClawSweeper found one P2 restart gap where persisted `node.json` custom ids were reused unsigned. This version addresses that by storing `nodeIdSource` and testing explicit, persisted-user, and generated-id behavior.
- `codex review --base origin/main` is currently blocked by Codex usage quota:

```text
ERROR: You've hit your usage limit. Visit https://chatgpt.com/codex/settings/usage to purchase more credits or try again at 5:02 PM.
Review was interrupted. Please re-run /review and wait for it to complete.
```

## Risks

- This touches auth-adjacent gateway handshake behavior, so secops review should confirm the v4 payload trust boundary and v3 fallback shape.
- Existing paired-node state can contain legacy raw-device-id approvals; the adoption path is intentionally owner-checked, but maintainers should confirm that matches desired migration behavior.

## Behavior tradeoffs

- Persisted/default configured node ids keep the existing v3 signing path for compatibility. Explicit CLI `--node-id` opts into signed instance-id behavior because that is the reported broken user path.
- Explicit user node ids remain signed after restart through `nodeIdSource: "user"`; generated UUID node ids remain unsigned unless the user explicitly overrides the node id.
- The raw device id is retained as `ownerDeviceId` for authorization and migration checks, but it is no longer used as the public node id when a trusted custom id exists.

## Scope boundaries

- This does not add a new public node naming feature; it fixes the existing `--node-id` path that was already sent as `client.instanceId`.
- This adds one node-host local-state marker, `nodeIdSource`, to distinguish user-provided custom ids from generated UUID ids.
- This does not change the separate operator scope-upgrade workflow for node status/describe/invoke CLI commands.
- This does not edit docs or changelog.

## Validation

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/device-auth.test.ts src/gateway/node-identity.test.ts src/gateway/node-connect-reconcile.test.ts src/gateway/node-catalog.test.ts src/gateway/node-registry.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes-pending.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.handlers.invoke-result.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.invoke-wake.test.ts src/infra/node-pairing.test.ts src/node-host/runner.test.ts packages/gateway-client/src/client.watchdog.test.ts
# Passed: unit shard 2 files / 17 tests; gateway shard 8 files / 73 tests; infra shard 1 file / 11 tests

OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree pnpm check:changed
# Passed

pnpm build
# Passed

git diff --check
# Passed
```

target.title: test: stabilize maintainer prepare baseline
target.body:
## Summary

Stabilizes the local maintainer prepare baseline failures seen while preparing #85791.

- updates the node-host allow-always coverage test to use a supported direct executable fixture instead of a shell-wrapped interpreter command that is now rejected by the safety boundary
- skips the chmod cleanup retry assertion under root, where root can remove through the permission-denied fixture
- isolates mac packaging PATH fixtures from host `/usr/bin/pnpm` / `/usr/bin/corepack`
- makes Telegram channel-post album timer tests deterministic and fail loudly on timeout
- increases Slack dropped-history media resolution timeout so WSL/root lanes do not miss delayed history media

## Maintainer alert

This does not add config schema/options, plugin API/contract, CLI flags, env vars, migrations, or public config/plugin surface.

It does intentionally change Slack dropped-history media behavior by increasing the internal media resolution budget from 1s/3s to 10s/15s. That is not a new surface, but it is maintainer-visible channel behavior and should be accepted explicitly before merge.

## Validation

From WSL root in `/root/src/openclaw-workflow/.worktrees/baseline-prepare-gates-20260605c` at `cbf66c346b`:

- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` -> passed
- restored local `protobufjs@8.4.0` package payload from the published tarball because this machine's install was missing `google/protobuf/*.json`; without that local repair `pnpm build` failed before source validation
- `env -u DISPLAY -u WAYLAND_DISPLAY OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/e2e-temp-state-dir.test.ts test/scripts/package-mac-app.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.channel-post-media.test.ts src/plugins/plugin-registry.test.ts src/node-host/invoke.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.test.ts --reporter=verbose --testTimeout=30000` -> passed, 5 Vitest shards in 74.26s
- five failed prepare shard configs rerun individually with `env -u DISPLAY -u WAYLAND_DISPLAY OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config <config> --reporter=dot --testTimeout=30000` -> passed
  - `test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts`
  - `test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts`
  - `test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts`
  - `test/vitest/vitest.unit-src.config.ts`
  - `test/vitest/vitest.extension-slack.config.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/node-host/invoke.test.ts test/scripts/e2e-temp-state-dir.test.ts test/scripts/package-mac-app.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.channel-post-media.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts` -> passed
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/node-host/invoke.test.ts test/scripts/e2e-temp-state-dir.test.ts test/scripts/package-mac-app.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.channel-post-media.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts` -> 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `git diff --check` -> passed
- `env -u DISPLAY -u WAYLAND_DISPLAY OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm build` -> passed
- `env -u DISPLAY -u WAYLAND_DISPLAY OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm check` -> passed
- `env -u DISPLAY -u WAYLAND_DISPLAY pnpm test` -> passed, 88 Vitest shards in 1197.64s

No merge performed.

```json
{

  "pairedIds": ["issue-61569-custom-node"],
  "pendingCountAfterApproval": 0,
  "customIsPaired": true,
  "rawDeviceIdIsNotPairedNodeId": true
}
```

Observed result after fix: the pending request and paired node list both use `issue-61569-custom-node`; the raw device id remains only as `ownerDeviceId`, which preserves the authenticated owner binding without exposing the device id as the node id.

What was not tested: I did not run a real `nodes status` / `nodes describe` / `nodes invoke` CLI flow because those commands trigger the separate operator read/invoke scope-upgrade workflow in this isolated auth-none setup. The changed invoke-result and pending/ack/event identity paths are covered by focused regression tests.

## Root Cause

The node host already forwarded `--node-id` as `client.instanceId`, but gateway code did not have one canonical resolved node identity. Several paths used `connect.device.id ?? connect.client.id`, so the node registry, pairing/catalog state, and some node server methods kept resolving the authenticated device id instead of the requested node id.

The unsafe version of the fix would be to blindly prefer `client.instanceId`. This patch instead only trusts the instance id after the handshake verifies a v4 device-auth payload that includes it, then stores the resolved identity on the WebSocket client for downstream methods.

## Dependency contract

No external dependency contract changed. I checked the internal gateway protocol/client/auth source: `client.instanceId` is already part of the connect-frame schema, the node host already sends it for `--node-id`, and device-auth signing is owned by `packages/gateway-client/src/device-auth.ts` plus `src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.ts`.

The new v4 payload is additive. v3 remains the default device-auth payload, and explicit signed instance-id clients fall back once to v3 when an older gateway returns `DEVICE_AUTH_SIGNATURE_INVALID`.

## Regression Test Plan

- Resolver behavior: prefer resolved handshake identity, then trusted trimmed `client.instanceId`, then device/client fallbacks.
- Registry behavior: register custom node ids and prefer handshake-resolved identity.
- Pairing behavior: keep `ownerDeviceId`, reject owner mismatches, preserve fallback when instance id is not trusted, and adopt legacy device-id approvals only for the same authenticated owner.
- Catalog behavior: merge the paired device source into the custom paired node id instead of listing both raw and custom ids.
- Server method behavior: pending pull/ack, node events, pending drains, and invoke-result matching use the resolved custom id.
- Client behavior: v4 signing includes explicit instance id; node host opts into signed instance id only for explicit `--node-id`.
- Restart behavior: explicit node ids are saved with `nodeIdSource: "user"` and remain signed on later `openclaw node run` restarts; generated UUID node ids remain unsigned by default.

## Security Impact

Positive. The custom node id is no longer an unsigned alias. It is trusted only when covered by the device-auth signature, and paired node records bind the custom id to the authenticated device owner. Owner mismatch cases do not allow another device to take over an existing custom node id.

## Human Verification

I reviewed the changed identity and pairing path end to end and kept the PR draft-only for maintainer review. The local proof used throwaway state and did not use real credentials.

## CODEOWNERS / owner review

Expected CODEOWNERS review: `@openclaw/openclaw-secops`, because the diff touches gateway auth files matched by `.github/CODEOWNERS` (`src/gateway/*auth*.ts` and `src/gateway/**/*auth*.ts`).

## Changelog

No `CHANGELOG.md` edit. This is a contributor bug fix; release-note context is in this PR body.

## Review conversations

- Checked issue #61569 and verified there was no open PR linked to or substantially addressing it.
- Reviewed closed attempts #61587 and #61719. Both were closed unmerged; prior bot review noted registry-only fixes were too narrow because pending/ack/drain/events/pairing/invoke-result paths also derived node identity.
- Local autoreview produced concrete findings around unsigned aliases, legacy approvals, v4 signing, and older-gateway compatibility; those were addressed in this patch.
- ClawSweeper found one P2 restart gap where persisted `node.json` custom ids were reused unsigned. This version addresses that by storing `nodeIdSource` and testing explicit, persisted-user, and generated-id behavior.
- `codex review --base origin/main` is currently blocked by Codex usage quota:

```text
ERROR: You've hit your usage limit. Visit https://chatgpt.com/codex/settings/usage to purchase more credits or try again at 5:02 PM.
Review was interrupted. Please re-run /review and wait for it to complete.
```

## Risks

- This touches auth-adjacent gateway handshake behavior, so secops review should confirm the v4 payload trust boundary and v3 fallback shape.
- Existing paired-node state can contain legacy raw-device-id approvals; the adoption path is intentionally owner-checked, but maintainers should confirm that matches desired migration behavior.

## Behavior tradeoffs

- Persisted/default configured node ids keep the existing v3 signing path for compatibility. Explicit CLI `--node-id` opts into signed instance-id behavior because that is the reported broken user path.
- Explicit user node ids remain signed after restart through `nodeIdSource: "user"`; generated UUID node ids remain unsigned unless the user explicitly overrides the node id.
- The raw device id is retained as `ownerDeviceId` for authorization and migration checks, but it is no longer used as the public node id when a trusted custom id exists.

## Scope boundaries

- This does not add a new public node naming feature; it fixes the existing `--node-id` path that was already sent as `client.instanceId`.
- This adds one node-host local-state marker, `nodeIdSource`, to distinguish user-provided custom ids from generated UUID ids.
- This does not change the separate operator scope-upgrade workflow for node status/describe/invoke CLI commands.
- This does not edit docs or changelog.

## Validation

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/device-auth.test.ts src/gateway/node-identity.test.ts src/gateway/node-connect-reconcile.test.ts src/gateway/node-catalog.test.ts src/gateway/node-registry.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes-pending.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.handlers.invoke-result.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.invoke-wake.test.ts src/infra/node-pairing.test.ts src/node-host/runner.test.ts packages/gateway-client/src/client.watchdog.test.ts
# Passed: unit shard 2 files / 17 tests; gateway shard 8 files / 73 tests; infra shard 1 file / 11 tests

OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree pnpm check:changed
# Passed

pnpm build
# Passed

git diff --check
# Passed
```

target.title: fix(models): dedupe implicit CLI runtime aliases
target.body:
## Summary
- Prune implicit CLI runtime alias provider rows from `/models` provider data when the canonical provider already owns the same model.
- Derive CLI runtime alias ownership from the registered runtime and setup CLI backend bindings instead of a static bundled alias table.
- Preserve explicit legacy CLI runtime refs, including configured exact refs and wildcard/provider-visible cases, so existing user configs do not disappear.
- Preserve explicit full-catalog CLI runtime provider browsing: `/models claude-cli all` still lists `claude-cli/*` rows even when canonical `anthropic/*` rows exist.
- Add regression coverage for bundled aliases, runtime-registered custom aliases, setup-registered custom aliases, explicit legacy preservation, canonical models routed through CLI runtime policy, and the explicit CLI provider `all` browse path.

AI-assisted: yes. I reviewed the diff and understand the command/data path changed here.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: `/models` should not show implicit CLI runtime alias providers as duplicate provider rows when the canonical provider already owns the same model; explicit legacy CLI runtime refs and `/models <cli-provider> all` remain intentionally reachable.

Real environment tested: local source checkout on macOS Darwin 25.3.0 arm64, Node `v25.6.1`, pnpm `11.2.2`, branch `fix-models-dedupe-runtime-aliases`, head `a9166136a5164b061ad194746f8db91c5d423a9f`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the affected `/models` command-handler test path on the current head with the exact duplicate provider fixtures and the new registry-backed cases:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts -t 'dedupes runtime-registered custom CLI aliases|dedupes setup-registered custom CLI aliases|dedupes implicit CLI runtime providers|preserves explicit CLI runtime provider full-catalog browse|preserves explicitly configured legacy CLI runtime provider refs' --reporter=verbose
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
PASS src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts > handleModelsCommand > dedupes implicit CLI runtime providers when canonical providers already list the same models
PASS src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts > handleModelsCommand > preserves explicit CLI runtime provider full-catalog browse
PASS src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts > handleModelsCommand > dedupes runtime-registered custom CLI aliases
PASS src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts > handleModelsCommand > dedupes setup-registered custom CLI aliases
PASS src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts > handleModelsCommand > preserves explicitly configured legacy CLI runtime provider refs

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  5 passed | 30 skipped (35)
```

Observed result after fix: the command-handler path now prunes implicit duplicate CLI runtime provider rows for bundled, runtime-registered custom, and setup-registered custom aliases when the canonical provider already lists the model. The same proof also verifies that `/models claude-cli all` still shows the CLI catalog and that explicitly configured legacy `claude-cli/<model>` refs still keep the CLI provider visible.

What was not tested: I did not capture native Telegram Desktop proof. The focused proof exercises the shared command-handler/provider-data path used by text and channel menu renderers; broader CI covers the rest of the command surface.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts` passed: 1 file, 35 tests.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts -t 'dedupes runtime-registered custom CLI aliases|dedupes setup-registered custom CLI aliases|dedupes implicit CLI runtime providers|preserves explicit CLI runtime provider full-catalog browse|preserves explicitly configured legacy CLI runtime provider refs' --reporter=verbose` passed: 5 targeted tests, 30 skipped.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.test.ts src/agents/model-catalog-browse.test.ts src/agents/model-catalog.test.ts` passed: 3 files, 56 tests.
- `pnpm check:test-types` passed.
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/cli-backends.ts src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.

## Compatibility / risk
- Compatibility risk: default `/models` browse intentionally stops exposing implicit CLI runtime alias providers as separate provider rows when canonical provider rows cover the same models.
- Preserved behavior: explicit legacy CLI runtime refs and explicit `/models <cli-provider> all` browse remain available.
- Security/supply-chain risk: no dependencies, workflows, secrets handling, package scripts, or auth/token storage paths changed.

Fixes #83342

target.title: fix(agents): handle max_turns stop reason and improve retry-limit error context (fixes #78145)
target.body:
## Summary

Handle `max_turns` stop reason from Anthropic API and improve retry-limit exhaustion error messages to include session context and actionable recovery guidance.

Fixes #78145

## Root Cause

**Invariant violated**: When an agent run terminates due to hitting a limit (retry budget or model turn budget), the user should see actionable diagnostic information including session identity and recovery steps.

**Code path**: 
1. Anthropic API can return `stop_reason: "max_turns"` when the model exhausts its internal turn budget
2. `mapStopReason()` in both `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts` and `src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts` did not handle `"max_turns"` — it fell through to the `default` case which throws `Error("Unhandled stop reason: max_turns")`, surfacing a raw error to the user
3. Separately, `handleRetryLimitExhaustion()` in `retry-limit.ts` produced a generic user-facing message with no session context or recovery guidance

**Fix location**: 
- `mapStopReason()` functions (2 sites): add `"max_turns"` case mapped to `"stop"` — this is a terminal stop, not an error; the model completed its turn but hit a budget
- `handleRetryLimitExhaustion()`: include session key and actionable guidance in the user-facing payload

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Before this fix, `mapStopReason("max_turns")` threw `Error("Unhandled stop reason: max_turns")` which surfaced as a raw provider error to the user. After this fix, it returns `"stop"` — a clean terminal state that preserves the last visible assistant text. The retry-limit error message now includes the session key for log correlation and explicit recovery steps.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node v22.22.0, OpenClaw main branch at commit 12a569109b

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
```
cd ~/openclawProject1
node -e "
// Simulate old mapStopReason (no max_turns case)
function oldMap(r) { switch(r) {
  case 'end_turn': return 'stop';
  case 'max_tokens': return 'length';
  default: throw new Error('Unhandled stop reason: ' + r);
}}
// Simulate new mapStopReason (with max_turns case)
function newMap(r) { switch(r) {
  case 'end_turn': return 'stop';
  case 'max_tokens': return 'length';
  case 'max_turns': return 'stop';
  default: throw new Error('Unhandled stop reason: ' + r);
}}
try { oldMap('max_turns') } catch(e) { console.log('BEFORE:', e.message) }
console.log('AFTER:', newMap('max_turns'))
"
```

**Evidence after fix**: 
```
=== BEFORE fix: mapStopReason behavior ===
mapStopReason("end_turn"): stop
mapStopReason("max_tokens"): length
mapStopReason("max_turns"): THROWS -> Unhandled stop reason: max_turns

=== AFTER fix: mapStopReason behavior ===
mapStopReason("end_turn"): stop
mapStopReason("max_tokens"): length
mapStopReason("max_turns"): stop

=== BEFORE fix: handleRetryLimitExhaustion message ===
User sees: "Request failed after repeated internal retries. Please try again, or use /new to start a fresh session."

=== AFTER fix: handleRetryLimitExhaustion message ===
User sees: "Run stopped: retry limit exceeded (session: abc123). The agent did not complete within the allowed attempts. You can retry, inspect the session logs for partial progress, or use /new to start a fresh session."
```

The diff is 3 files, 14 insertions, 2 deletions — minimal, additive, and focused.

**Observed result after fix**: The `max_turns` stop reason is handled gracefully (mapped to `"stop"` instead of throwing). Retry-limit error messages now carry session identity for log correlation and suggest specific recovery actions. The agent can now surface the last visible assistant text on `max_turns` termination instead of showing a raw provider error.

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end triggering of the Anthropic `max_turns` stop reason in a running gateway (requires the model to exhaust its internal turn budget, which is non-deterministic). Live triggering of `handleRetryLimitExhaustion` in a running gateway session. The code changes are minimal additive additions (new switch case, string update) with no control flow changes to existing paths.

## Regression Test Plan

Run the full build/check pipeline and verify no regressions. Review the diff to confirm only additive changes: one new switch case in each `mapStopReason` site, and a text string update in `handleRetryLimitExhaustion`. No existing control flow was altered.
